## Research Plan `pl_003` — Q001: Who were Patrick Flynn's parents?

*Picking up after completed `pl_002` (1860 census, death cert, FamilySearch probate → negative). Census coverage is treated as done.*

---

### Step 1 — `pli_007` · Church records · FamilySearch · *Start here*
**Collection:** "Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985" (collection 1401638, indexed, free)
**Target:** Thomas Flynn's marriage in Schuylkill County, ca. 1840–1870

This is the dedicated parents'-marriage item required by every parentage plan. The 1850 and 1860 censuses name Thomas as head of household but **never name his wife** — and no other source in hand does either. A marriage register entry would (a) confirm Thomas and his wife as a married couple, (b) supply the **mother's maiden name**, and (c) by its date relative to Patrick's ~1845 birth, corroborate Thomas as the father named so far only by indirect census evidence and a secondary death-certificate informant.

---

### Step 2 — `pli_008` · Civil/compiled marriage indexes · FamilySearch
**Collections:** "Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950" (1571957) + "Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900" (1502909)
**Target:** Thomas Flynn's marriage — different index base from pli_007

Covers county marriage bonds, licenses, and registers not necessarily in the church-records collection. Pre-1868, Schuylkill County Catholic parishes fell under the Diocese of Philadelphia — coverage across collections is uneven, so casting a wider net across both is worth the effort. Both are free on FamilySearch.

---

### Step 3 — `pli_009` · Probate · Ancestry
**Target:** Thomas Flynn will or letters of administration, Schuylkill County, broader window 1840–1910

FamilySearch (pli_006) returned nothing for 1870–1900. Ancestry has independent indexing of Pennsylvania Register of Wills records with different OCR and date-range coverage. Thomas may have died before 1870 or after 1900, outside the window already searched. A will naming Patrick as a son would be **direct evidence of parentage** — the strongest single record type available for this question.

---

### Step 4 — `pli_010` · Immigration records · FamilySearch
**Target:** Thomas Flynn ship manifest from Ireland, ca. 1840–1849

Patrick was born in Ireland (~1845) but is in Schuylkill County by 1850 — the family emigrated in that window. A passenger list showing Thomas traveling with a wife and young child would independently identify the mother's name and confirm the family unit. Also search phonetic variants (Flinn, Flin).

---

### Step 5 — `pli_011` · Land records · FamilySearch
**Target:** Thomas Flynn deeds, Schuylkill County grantor/grantee indexes, 1845–1900

Under Pennsylvania law, a wife's **dower right** required her to join every conveyance of real property — so deed records are one of the only pre-1906 non-church sources that can name the mother directly. Deed witnesses also constitute the FAN cluster (BCG Standard 14).

---

### Step 6 — `pli_012` · Diocesan archives (offline) · *Fallback for pli_007*
**Repositories:** Diocese of Scranton Archives (post-1868) + Philadelphia Archdiocesan Historical Research Center (pre-1868)

The localities guide notes that Catholic sacramental registers are held at the parish or diocesan level and that some coal-region films are browse-only or not indexed at all online. If the FamilySearch indexed search (pli_007) finds nothing, the original registers at the diocesan archives are the authoritative fallback. Requires a written request or in-person visit.

---

`pli_012` (diocesan archives) falls back from `pli_007` (FamilySearch church records) — if step 1 finds Thomas's marriage online, step 6 can be skipped.

**6 searches** across 4 record categories (church × 3, probate, immigration, land). Covers free repositories first, paid second, offline last.

## FAN-Based Parentage Plan — Patrick Flynn (q_001)

**Why a FAN plan now:** The two prior plans (pl_001, pl_002) completed direct searches of the 1850 and 1860 censuses, Patrick's death certificate, and Schuylkill County probate — all pointing to Thomas Flynn but none providing proved, direct parentage evidence. Three census years remain unsearched, the mother is completely unidentified, and no records of Patrick's siblings, associates, or church community have been consulted. This plan attacks those gaps through the FAN cluster.

---

### The 9 Items in Execution Order

| # | ID | Record Type | Target | Repository | Rationale Summary |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | pli_007 | Census | Schuylkill Co., 1870 | FamilySearch | First census gap; search Patrick + Thomas + **neighbors** (FAN cluster of Irish households) |
| 2 | pli_008 | Census | Schuylkill Co., 1880 | FamilySearch | **First census with relationship column** — could explicitly state "son"; enumerate Thomas's other children for sibling names |
| 3 | pli_009 | Census | Schuylkill Co., 1900 | FamilySearch | Final census gap; 1900 uniquely asks **father's and mother's birthplace** |
| 4 | pli_010 | Marriage (parents') | Schuylkill Co., 1835–1855 | FamilySearch | **Thomas Flynn's marriage** — the only way to learn the mother's maiden name; uses Civil Marriages 1677–1950 + Compiled Marriages 1700–1900 |
| 5 | pli_011 | Marriage (parents') | Schuylkill Co., 1835–1855 | Ancestry | *Fallback for pli_010* — county license books Ancestry holds independently |
| 6 | pli_012 | Church | Schuylkill Co., 1845–1865 | FamilySearch | Baptism/confirmation of Patrick or siblings in Catholic parishes — names both parents; **godparents** are a classic FAN source; some registers browse-only per locality guide |
| 7 | pli_013 | Death certs (siblings) | Schuylkill Co., 1906–1930 | FamilySearch | Patrick's **siblings' death certificates** name the same parents — independent corroboration and supplies mother's name; execute *after* censuses identify sibling names |
| 8 | pli_014 | Court / Naturalization | Schuylkill Co., 1840–1880 | FamilySearch | Thomas Flynn's naturalization petition may name his wife; Patrick's confirms identity and may cite his father |
| 9 | pli_015 | Newspaper | Schuylkill Co., 1870–1910 | Other (Newspapers.com / Chronicling America) | Patrick's **1908 obituary** may name siblings → leads for pli_013; Thomas's obituary, if found, may name wife and children directly |

---

### Key FAN Logic

- **pli_007–009 (censuses)** don't just fill timeline gaps — the neighbors in each enumeration district are the FAN cluster. Isolate the Irish-born households within a few pages of Thomas Flynn in 1870 and 1880.
- **pli_010/011 (marriage)** is the single highest-value new record type: no source searched so far has named the mother. Her maiden name unlocks pli_012 and pli_013.
- **pli_012 (church) + pli_013 (sibling death certs)** are parallel FAN evidence paths — either could independently name both parents from a different informant than the son-in-law who reported on the death certificate.
- **pli_015 (obituary) → pli_013 (sibling death certs)** is a sequenced dependency: find sibling names in the obituary, then search their death certificates.

**Fallback relationship:** If pli_010 (FamilySearch marriage) yields nothing → pli_011 (Ancestry marriage bonds).

**Total scope:** 9 searches. FamilySearch items (pli_007–014) can be executed by `search-records`; pli_015 (obituaries) via `search-external-sites`.

---

Ready to start? The natural first step is **pli_007 — the 1870 census** on FamilySearch, searching both Patrick Flynn and Thomas Flynn simultaneously and logging the neighbors. Say "execute the plan" to begin.

entry.rationale: The 1870 census is the first of three census gaps flagged as high-severity in the project timeline (t_001) and noted in the existing proof summary (ps_001) as the primary remaining gap. Search for Patrick Flynn (age ~25) and separately for Thomas Flynn (candidate father, age ~52). Neighbors enumerated in the same pages are a primary FAN source: identifying Irish households clustered near Thomas Flynn may reveal relatives, associates, or members of the same townland community who hold corroborating information about his family. If Thomas Flynn no longer appears in the county, that shapes the search for his death and probate.

The 1880 census is the first federal enumeration to include an explicit relationship column — making it the earliest census that could state 'son' or 'father' in print rather than requiring inference from household position. If Thomas Flynn (age ~62) is still living and Patrick (age ~35) remains in his household, this is potentially the strongest direct census evidence of the parent-child relationship yet available. Even if Patrick heads his own household, enumerate Thomas's household for other adult children (Patrick's siblings) whose names supply leads for the death-certificate and church-record FAN searches below. Neighbors of both Thomas and Patrick are a secondary FAN resource.

Patrick Flynn (age ~55) should appear in the 1900 census, which uniquely asks for the birthplace of each person's mother and father. Self-reported or household-reported parental birthplaces are indirect evidence but provide a new factual layer not present in any source searched so far. Completes the third census gap noted in ps_001. Also enumerate neighbors: by 1900, Patrick's children and any surviving siblings form a close FAN cluster that may point to additional record leads.

Search FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950' (col. 1571957) and 'Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900' (col. 1502909) for Thomas Flynn's marriage. This is the dedicated parents'-marriage item required by the record-type guide for any parentage plan. Its three contributions: (a) identifies Patrick's mother by her maiden name — a name no record searched so far has supplied; (b) confirms Thomas and his wife as a married couple and dates their union relative to Patrick's ~1845 birth, corroborating Thomas as the father; (c) supplies the wife's name for the church-record and sibling-death-certificate searches below. Thomas (born ~1818) likely married in the late 1830s or early 1840s, either in Ireland before emigrating or in Schuylkill County soon after arrival. Note: this record proves the couple married, not that Patrick is their child — a record placing Patrick with them (census, baptism, or probate) remains necessary for the parentage conclusion.

Fallback for pli_010. If FamilySearch's Pennsylvania marriage collections do not index Thomas Flynn's marriage — likely because it predates statewide civil registration or is an Irish immigrant church marriage not yet indexed — search Ancestry's Pennsylvania marriage collections and Schuylkill County marriage bond/license books. Ancestry holds some county-level license registers not yet on FamilySearch and has independent indexing that may pick up variant spellings of Flynn.

Search FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985' (col. 1401638) for sacramental registers of Schuylkill County Catholic parishes. Two FAN angles: (1) A baptismal or confirmation record for Patrick Flynn (ca. 1845–1860) names both parents — direct parentage evidence of a type no record searched so far has provided. (2) Baptism records of Patrick's siblings (other Flynn children baptized by Thomas and his wife) independently name the same parents and supply the mother's maiden name. Godparents named in baptism records are a primary FAN source: in Irish Catholic practice, godparents were typically siblings, cousins, or close community associates of the parents. Per the locality guide: Schuylkill County parishes fell under the Diocese of Philadelphia until 1868; some anthracite-region registers are browse-only image groups (~0% record-searchable per the quirks note), so plan to browse image-by-image if the indexed search returns nothing.

Search FamilySearch 'Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968' (col. 2255317) for death certificates of Thomas Flynn's other children — Patrick's siblings identified in the 1870, 1880, or 1900 census searches. Pennsylvania statewide civil death registration began in 1906; any sibling who died in Schuylkill County after that date would have a certificate naming both parents. A sibling's death certificate naming Thomas Flynn as father and supplying the mother's full maiden name is independent corroboration of the parentage conclusion, drawn from a separate informant's knowledge. This is a textbook FAN approach: the subject's siblings' own records contain evidence about the shared parents. Execute after the census searches (pli_007–pli_009) have identified sibling names to search.

Search FamilySearch for naturalization records of Thomas Flynn and Patrick Flynn filed in Schuylkill County's Court of Common Pleas. As Irish immigrants, both would have naturalized. Thomas Flynn's declaration of intention or petition names his country of origin, arrival date, and sometimes his wife — FAN evidence linking the family unit and potentially corroborating the marriage record. Patrick Flynn's own petition confirms his identity and may name his father or birthplace. Pre-1906 Pennsylvania naturalizations were processed through county courts and are indexed in part on FamilySearch; some volumes are browse-only.

Search for Patrick Flynn's 1908 obituary and Thomas Flynn's obituary (approximate date unknown, likely 1870–1900) in Schuylkill County newspapers — the Miners' Journal (Pottsville) and the Pottsville Republican — via Newspapers.com, Chronicling America (Library of Congress), and the Schuylkill County Historical Society. Obituaries from this period routinely name surviving children, siblings, and sometimes parents: Patrick's obituary may identify surviving siblings (leads for pli_013) or name Thomas Flynn directly as his father. Thomas Flynn's obituary, if found, may name his wife, their children, and his Irish origin — a FAN document that could supply the mother's name and independently confirm the parent-child relationship. Execute via search-external-sites skill.

## Research Plan — `pl_003` · Question `q_001`
**Post-1860 census tracking of Patrick Flynn; Thomas Flynn co-residence**

---

### Why this plan now
The prior plan (`pl_002`) is **completed**: the 1860 census, death certificate, and probate search are all done. The existing proof summary is rated only **"Probable"** because both censuses provide *indirect* parentage evidence (no relationship column before 1880) and the death certificate informant is secondary. The proof summary itself calls out 1870, 1880, and 1900 as the primary remaining gap. This plan closes it.

---

### Execution order

| # | ID | Census | Repository | Subject searched | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `pli_007` | **1880** | FamilySearch (free) | Patrick Flynn (~35) | **Highest priority.** The 1880 census is the *first* to record each person's relationship to the head. If Thomas (~62) is living with Patrick, the relationship column supplies **direct** evidence of father-son parentage — the only corroborated-direct evidence the case currently lacks. |
| 2 | `pli_008` | **1870** | FamilySearch (free) | Patrick Flynn (~25) | Bridges the gap from 1860. At 25, Patrick may still be in Thomas's (~52) household or newly established nearby. Either result is informative. |
| 3 | `pli_009` | **1870** | FamilySearch (free) | **Thomas Flynn (~52) as head** | FAN search. If Patrick has left, Thomas's own household may name other Flynn children who can independently corroborate the parentage claim through their own records. Also confirms Thomas was alive in 1870, setting up the 1880 opportunity. |
| 4 | `pli_010` | **1900** | FamilySearch (free) | Patrick Flynn (~55) | Thomas would be ~82 — elderly but not impossible. If present in Patrick's household, the 1900 relationship column (carried forward from 1880) again provides direct evidence. Even absent, finding Patrick's own established household confirms continuity to his 1908 death. |
| 5 | `pli_011` | 1880 | **Ancestry** (paid) | Patrick Flynn | *Fallback for `pli_007`.* Independent index — catches different spelling reads (Flyn, Flinn, Glynn) that a FamilySearch-only search might miss. |

The 1890 federal census was almost entirely destroyed in a 1921 fire — only fragments survive for a handful of states, and Pennsylvania is not among them. It is correctly omitted.

entry.rationale: The 1880 census is the highest-priority search for this parentage question because it is the first federal census to record each person's relationship to the head of household. Patrick Flynn (~35) should appear in Schuylkill County. If Thomas Flynn (~62) is in the same household — as an aging parent living with his adult son — the relationship column would supply direct evidence of the father-son connection that the 1850 and 1860 censuses (which lack that column) could only imply. Indexed in FamilySearch collection 1417683 ('United States Census, 1880'). Free access; search first.

The 1870 census tracks Patrick Flynn (~25) in the decade immediately after he was last seen in Thomas Flynn's household (1860). At 25, Patrick may still be residing with Thomas (~52) or may have established his own household nearby. Either result is valuable: continued co-residence is additional indirect evidence of parentage; a new household may reveal a wife or neighbors who connect back to Thomas. Does not have a relationship column (pre-1880), but household co-residence with Thomas would still constitute indirect evidence. Indexed in FamilySearch collection 1438024 ('United States Census, 1870'). Free access.

FAN-cluster search: search Thomas Flynn (~52) as the subject rather than Patrick, in case Patrick has left the household. If Thomas has his own 1870 household entry, it may name other Flynn children still at home — siblings or half-siblings of Patrick — who can corroborate the parentage relationship through their own records. Also confirms Thomas was still alive in 1870 (critical for evaluating the 1880 census opportunity). Same FamilySearch collection 1438024. Run concurrently with or immediately after pli_008.

The 1900 census tracks Patrick Flynn (~55) at the end of his documented lifespan (he died in 1908). Thomas Flynn would be approximately 82 in 1900 — elderly but not implausibly alive. If Thomas appears as a dependent in Patrick's household, the 1900 relationship column (introduced 1880, continued 1900) would supply direct evidence of the father-son relationship. Even if Thomas is absent (likely deceased by then), finding Patrick's own household confirms the research subject's continuity and may identify children or other family members who link back to Thomas. Indexed in FamilySearch collection 1325221 ('United States Census, 1900'). Free access.

Fallback for pli_007: if FamilySearch returns no results for Patrick Flynn in the 1880 census, search Ancestry's independently created index of the same original census. Ancestry's transcribers work separately from FamilySearch's and sometimes read a surname differently (e.g., 'Flyn,' 'Flinn,' 'Glynn') or index a different spelling that missed the FamilySearch index. The underlying original images are identical, but the index is independent — a missed result on one platform may surface on the other. Paid repository; run only if pli_007 is negative or inconclusive.

Fallback for pli_008: if FamilySearch returns no results for Patrick Flynn in the 1870 census, search Ancestry's independent index. Spelling variation risk is slightly higher in 1870 than 1880 because the 1870 census was enumerated earlier and some Schuylkill County enumerators recorded immigrant names phonetically. Ancestry's broader fuzzy-name matching may surface results that a strict FamilySearch index search misses. Paid repository; run only if pli_008 is negative or inconclusive.

Fallback for pli_010: if FamilySearch returns no results for Patrick Flynn in the 1900 census, search Ancestry's independent index. By 1900, Patrick (~55) is a well-established adult who should be easy to find; a negative result on both platforms would warrant a broader geographic search (adjacent counties) rather than continued Schuylkill-only searching. Paid repository; run only if pli_010 is negative or inconclusive.

## Research Plan `pl_002` — q_001: Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845, d. 1908)?

**Starting-point check:** The only evidence in hand is the 1850 census co-enumeration of Patrick (age 5) in Thomas Flynn's Schuylkill County household. That is **indirect evidence only** — the 1850 census states no relationships. The active hypothesis (`h_001`) that Thomas is Patrick's father is supported but unproven. Every item below is designed to find direct or corroborating evidence.

**Note:** The project objective says Patrick was "born in Pennsylvania," but the 1850 census asserts birthplace "Ireland." Several items below may resolve that discrepancy.

---

### Execution order

entry.rationale: Patrick Flynn died 12 March 1908 in Schuylkill County — after Pennsylvania statewide civil death registration took effect in 1906. His death certificate is almost certain to exist and is the highest-probability direct source for parentage: the informant (typically a family member) is expected to name Patrick's father and mother. FamilySearch holds the Pennsylvania Death Certificates 1906-1968 collection (indexed). The certificate may also clarify the birthplace discrepancy between the project objective ('Pennsylvania') and the 1850 census assertion ('Ireland').

Ancestry independently indexes the Pennsylvania death certificates; its index may surface results where FamilySearch's misses due to transcription differences in the surname. Search here if pli_004 (FamilySearch) yields nothing or if the FamilySearch image is unclear.

Patrick Flynn (b. ~1845) would be approximately 15 in the 1860 census — old enough to appear by name but likely still residing in the parental household. A second co-enumeration in Thomas Flynn's household strengthens the parentage hypothesis and may also yield the wife/mother's given name, which was not extracted from the 1850 census assertions. The 1860 census is fully indexed on FamilySearch.

If Thomas Flynn (b. ~1818) predeceased Patrick and left a will, it is direct parentage evidence — naming Patrick as a son and heir. Per the locality guide, Schuylkill County probate is held by the Register of Wills from 1811 and appears on FamilySearch as 'Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683-1994' (collection 1417683). The county browse-only volume (IGN 007936749, Schuylkill Probate 1851-1930, 0% record-searchable) must be browsed image-by-image alongside any indexed results. An administration record (if Thomas died intestate) would also name next-of-kin heirs.

The Flynn family were almost certainly Irish Catholic, typical of the anthracite coal community in Schuylkill County. Sacramental registers — baptisms, confirmations, First Communions — from local Catholic parishes may name Thomas (and his wife) as parents of Patrick or siblings. FamilySearch collection 1401638 ('Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985') is partially indexed; the county browse-only film (IGN 007936751, Schuylkill Church Records 1842-1895, 0% record-searchable) covers the critical period and requires image-by-image review. Per the locality guide, parishes fell under the Diocese of Philadelphia until 1868, when the Diocese of Scranton was erected; originals are held at the parish or diocesan archives.

entry.jurisdiction: Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania; County of origin, Ireland
entry.rationale: Dedicated search for the marriage of Thomas Flynn to Patrick's mother — a separate plan item required for any parentage plan. The couple's marriage record: (a) confirms Thomas and his wife as a married unit; (b) supplies the mother's maiden name, which census and death records typically omit; and (c) corroborates Thomas as father by placing the marriage before Patrick's ~1845 birth. Note the limit: the marriage proves they wed and dates the union but is not, by itself, evidence that Patrick is their child — that requires a record placing Patrick with them (the 1850 census household, or ideally a baptism). Thomas (b. ~1818) most likely married in Ireland ca. 1840-1844 before emigrating, so Irish Catholic marriage registers are the primary target; Pennsylvania Catholic records (collection 1401638) and the Schuylkill browse-only marriage volume (IGN 007936753, 1870-1910) are secondary checks.

Patrick Flynn (~25 in 1870, ~35 in 1880) is likely heading his own household by these enumerations. The 1880 census records each person's father's birthplace and mother's birthplace in separate columns — indirect but corroborating evidence for Thomas's identity as father and for the mother's origin. FamilySearch holds fully indexed versions of both censuses. Also check whether Flynn siblings from the 1860 household appear in the same neighborhood as a FAN-cluster cross-check.

Patrick Flynn was born in Ireland ca. 1845 (per census assertion a_002) and appeared in Schuylkill County by 1850 — the family emigrated within roughly five years of Patrick's birth. A passenger list showing Thomas Flynn arriving with wife and an infant or young child named Patrick is indirect parentage evidence, placing the family as a unit before their first American record. FamilySearch indexes pre-1891 passenger manifests. Finding the family on a single manifest also helps resolve the birthplace discrepancy (project objective says 'Pennsylvania'; the census says 'Ireland').

Schuylkill County newspapers (Miners' Journal, Pottsville Republican) regularly published obituaries and death notices naming the deceased's parents, surviving children, and place of origin. An obituary could directly name Patrick's parents or confirm Thomas Flynn as his father. Search Newspapers.com and GenealogyBank for 'Patrick Flynn' in Schuylkill County ca. March 1908. Also search for Thomas Flynn's obituary (date unknown), which may name Patrick among survivors.

## Research Plan `pl_003` — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn? (`q_001`)

**Context:** Two prior plans are complete. The proof summary (`ps_001`) rates the conclusion **Probable** — two censuses and a death certificate all point to Thomas Flynn as father, but the mother is completely unidentified, three censuses are unsearched, and probate was only tried on FamilySearch. This plan closes those gaps.

---

### Execution order

| # | Item | Record type | Repository | What it could reveal |

| 2 | **`pli_008`** — 1870 U.S. Census | Census | FamilySearch | Third consecutive enumeration in Thomas Flynn's household corroborates the relationship; mother named in same record. |
| 3 | **`pli_009`** — 1900 U.S. Census | Census | FamilySearch | Closes the 48-year gap (1860–1908) flagged in `ps_001`; years of immigration and mother tongue can confirm Irish origin. |

| 5 | **`pli_011`** — Civil/compiled marriages *(fallback for pli_010)* | Vital record | FamilySearch (colls. 1571957, 1502909) | Alternative index if church records yield nothing — county bond/license records and compiled marriage indexes. |
| 6 | **`pli_012`** — Thomas Flynn probate | Probate | Ancestry | FamilySearch was negative (`log_006`). Ancestry's Pennsylvania probate holdings may surface a record not indexed there; a will naming Patrick as son = direct primary evidence. |
| 7 | **`pli_013`** — Thomas Flynn naturalization papers | Immigration | FamilySearch | Declarations and petitions often name the petitioner's place of origin and sometimes a spouse — another path to the mother's name. Note: locality guide warns some Schuylkill-region naturalization films are browse-only. |
| 8 | **`pli_014`** — FAN cluster: Flynn siblings 1870–1900 | Census | FamilySearch | Patrick's likely siblings in the 1850/1860 Thomas Flynn household. Their death certificates independently name Thomas as father; corroborating parentage from a collateral line. |

entry.rationale: The 1880 census introduced the relationship column, so any listing of Patrick (age ~35) in Thomas Flynn's household would explicitly state 'son' — direct evidence of parentage rather than the inferred co-residence used in pl_002. Thomas Flynn's wife would also be named in the same enumeration, identifying Patrick's mother, whose name is currently unknown from any searched source. Free and indexed on FamilySearch; highest probability of advancing the conclusion from Probable to Proved.

Patrick would be approximately 25 in 1870 and may still reside with Thomas Flynn or nearby in Schuylkill County. Although the 1870 census lacks the relationship column, a third consecutive enumeration in the same household would corroborate the parent-child relationship inferred from the 1850 and 1860 censuses. Thomas Flynn's wife (Patrick's presumed mother) would be named, filling the critical gap on the mother's identity.

Patrick would be approximately 55 in 1900, eight years before his 1908 death. The 1900 enumeration closes the 48-year timeline gap (1860–1908) flagged in ps_001, and shows his household, occupation, and years of immigration. The 1900 census records years of immigration and mother tongue, which may help confirm Irish origin and distinguish him from other Patrick Flynns in the county.

Dedicated parentage item for the candidate parents' marriage to each other. Thomas Flynn (~b. 1818, Ireland) married before Patrick's birth (~1845); the marriage record would supply the mother's maiden name — the single most critical missing fact in this case, absent from every source searched. Roman Catholic parishes in Schuylkill County fell under the Diocese of Philadelphia until 1868. FamilySearch collection 1401638 ('Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985') indexes and images Catholic sacramental registers for this area and period. The marriage record also corroborates Thomas Flynn's candidacy as father by its date relative to Patrick's birth.

The FamilySearch search of the Schuylkill County Register of Wills (1870–1900) was negative (log_006). Ancestry's Pennsylvania probate holdings draw from the same Register of Wills records and may surface a Thomas Flynn probate not indexed on FamilySearch, or capture a slightly wider date range. A will naming Patrick as a son would be direct, primary-information evidence of parentage — the single most probative record type for this question.

Thomas Flynn emigrated from Ireland, likely in the mid-1840s given that Patrick was born in Ireland (~1845) and the family was in Schuylkill County by 1850. Naturalization declarations and final petitions filed in Pennsylvania courts often name the petitioner's place of origin and sometimes a spouse or witnesses. If Thomas Flynn's papers name his wife, they would supply the mother's maiden name. Siblings who established their own households by 1870 would appear as household heads in later censuses with the relationship column; their death certificates would independently name Thomas Flynn as father, supplying corroborating parentage evidence from a collateral line. Search for adult Flynns in Schuylkill County 1870–1900 whose ages in 1850 are consistent with being Thomas Flynn's children.

**Subject:** Mikkel Nielsen Blyeberg, b. 20 Aug 1779, Stagstrup Sogn, Thisted Amt, Denmark. Patronymic "Nielsen" points to a father named *Niels* — but which Niels is unknown.

**Key constraints:** The Stagstrup church book is only 3% indexed and both the levy rolls and probate are 0% indexed. Most items require browsing images rather than name searches.

Here is the current research plan for the parentage question (**q_001**: "Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn, b. ~1845, PA, d. 1908?").

---

## Active Plan — pl_002

**Question:** q_001 — Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn?
**Plan status:** Active | 2 of 3 items completed; 1 in progress

---

### Step 1 — 1860 U.S. Census, Schuylkill County, PA (FamilySearch) ✅ Completed
**Rationale:** Confirm Patrick still in the Thomas Flynn household in 1860, strengthening the parent-child identification across two census years.
**Result:** Patrick Flynn, age 15, found in the Thomas Flynn household (dwelling 112, family 119) — consistent with the 1850 enumeration.

---

### Step 2 — Death Certificate, Schuylkill County, PA, 1908 (FamilySearch) ✅ Completed
**Rationale:** Patrick's death certificate may name his parents directly.
**Result:** Certificate no. 4521 found; informant James Brown (son-in-law) named **Thomas Flynn** as Patrick's father. This is direct evidence, though from a secondary informant.

---

### Step 3 — Probate Records, Schuylkill County, PA, 1870–1890 (FamilySearch) 🔄 In Progress
**Rationale:** Thomas Flynn's will or probate record may name Patrick as a son — the strongest corroboration available if found.
**Status:** Search currently underway.

---

## What's Next

**Complete pli_006** — finish the probate search currently in progress. A will naming Patrick as a son would elevate the conclusion from *Probable* to *Proved* (the existing proof summary ps_001 explicitly flags this as the remaining gap).

The proof summary also notes that the **1870, 1880, and 1900 censuses** have not yet been searched. Those are not in the current plan. Once the probate search concludes, a second plan pass may be warranted to extend coverage to those census years and firm up research exhaustiveness.

---

**Estimated remaining scope:** 1 active search (probate, pli_006). Would you like me to execute it, or extend the plan to cover the missing census years first?

output.text_response:
Good. I can see the project clearly. Here's what I'm working with:

- **Active plan `pl_002`** for question `q_001` (parents of Patrick Flynn)
- Two items already **completed**: `pli_004` (1860 census) and `pli_005` (1908 death certificate)
- One item still **in_progress**: `pli_006` — probate search with `date_range: "1870-1890"`

The new reference shifts Thomas Flynn's estimated death to ~1865, making `pli_006`'s window wrong and warranting a **supersede** of `pl_002` and a fresh plan targeting 1860–1870. Let me first check what collections and external resources cover Pennsylvania in that revised window.Good. I have what I need. The collections search confirms **Pennsylvania Probate Records, 1683–1994** (FamilySearch collection 1417683) and **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** (collection 1401638) both cover the revised 1860–1870 window. External links returned nothing useful for this period in Schuylkill County.

Now I'll execute the re-plan in one atomic batch:
1. Supersede `pl_002` (its open `pli_006` assumed a 1870-1890 death window that the new reference overturns)
2. Create `pl_003` with five items targeting 1860–1870 — the corrected window for Thomas Flynn's probable deathThe plan has been revised successfully. Here's a summary of what changed and what the new plan looks like:

---

## What Changed

**`pl_002` → superseded.** Its open item `pli_006` (probate, date range `"1870-1890"`) was built on the assumption that Thomas Flynn died in the 1875–1890 window. The new reference overturns that assumption, so the entire plan is retired as an audit record and replaced.

---

## New Plan: `pl_003` — Thomas Flynn's Death, ca. 1865 (5 items)

| # | ID | Record Type | Date Range | Repository | Notes |
|---|-----|-------------|------------|------------|-------|
| 1 | `pli_007` | Probate | **1860–1870** | FamilySearch | Primary target — PA Probate Records collection 1417683; will naming Patrick as son would be direct parentage evidence. Also flags that `src_006`'s 1881 will falls outside the revised window and may belong to a different Thomas Flynn. |
| 2 | `pli_008` | Probate | **1860–1870** | Ancestry | *Fallback for `pli_007`* — independent index and images if FamilySearch yields nothing. |
| 3 | `pli_009` | Census | 1870 | FamilySearch | **Verification step** — Thomas's absence from the 1870 census corroborates a pre-1870 death; his presence would push the window back and require re-dating the probate items. |
| 4 | `pli_010` | Church | **1860–1870** | FamilySearch | Catholic burial/death registers (collection 1401638); St. Mary's Pottsville most likely parish given Patrick's baptism there. May name survivors. |
| 5 | `pli_011` | Tax | **1860–1870** | County/State Archives | Annual tax rolls — Thomas's last year of appearance brackets his death date from above, directly testing the ca. 1865 estimate. |

---

## Recommended Execution Order

**Start with `pli_009` (1870 census)** — it's the cheapest test of the new hypothesis. If Thomas Flynn appears in 1870, the revised death estimate is wrong and we'd need to re-examine the reference before searching probate. If he's absent, proceed to `pli_007` (probate on FamilySearch). If that yields nothing, try `pli_008` (Ancestry) as the fallback.

Would you like me to begin executing the plan — starting with the 1870 census check for Thomas Flynn?

entry.rationale: A new reference places Thomas Flynn's death ca. 1865, shifting the search window from 1870-1890 to 1860-1870. Probate proceedings typically follow death by 1-3 years. FamilySearch collection 1417683 (Pennsylvania Probate Records, 1683-1994) is indexed and free — search here first. A will naming Patrick as a son would be direct evidence of parentage. Note: src_006 already cites a will dated 1881, which falls outside the revised window and may belong to a different Thomas Flynn — this search will clarify whether an earlier probate exists.

Ancestry holds Pennsylvania probate records independently of FamilySearch. If the FamilySearch search (pli_007) yields nothing or an incomplete image, Ancestry provides a separate index and image set covering the same Schuylkill County Register of Wills for the 1860-1870 period.

If Thomas Flynn died ca. 1865, he should be absent from the 1870 census. Searching the 1870 Schuylkill County census directly tests the revised death-date estimate: absence corroborates a pre-1870 death and validates the 1860-1870 probate window; presence would contradict it and require pushing the search window later. This is a low-cost verification step that governs whether the probate items need date-range adjustment.

Thomas Flynn was likely Catholic (Irish immigrant, established Catholic community in Schuylkill County's mining region). Catholic parish death and burial registers in FamilySearch collection 1401638 (Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708-1985) may record Thomas's death or burial, corroborating or refining the ca. 1865 estimate and potentially naming survivors. St. Mary's, Pottsville is the most likely parish given Patrick's baptism there (src_005).

Schuylkill County annual tax assessment lists track taxable individuals year by year. Thomas Flynn's last year of appearance in the tax rolls brackets his death date from above, directly testing the ca. 1865 estimate. These records are held at the Schuylkill County courthouse and may also be available through the Pennsylvania State Archives or on FamilySearch as browse-only images. Disappearance from tax rolls in a specific year narrows the probate search and corroborates (or contradicts) the new reference's 1865 estimate.
